title: Bullwhip Effect

content.statement: Small swings in customer demand amplify into huge swings the further upstream you go.
content.explainer: Each tier in a supply chain over-reacts to the tier below it, inflating orders to protect itself, so a tiny blip at the customer end becomes a violent surge at the factory. Recognizing it stops you from mistaking noise for a real trend.
questions: Are your upstream orders swinging far harder than your actual end-customer demand is?
How many tiers sit between your real demand signal and the decisions your suppliers make?
Are you reacting to a genuine shift in demand, or amplifying normal noise into a panic order?
sop: Measure your true end-customer demand swing, the percentage it actually moves week to week (see calculation, page 6).
Count the tiers between that real demand and your furthest-upstream supplier (see calculation, page 6).
Estimate how much the swing amplifies across those tiers to see the surge your suppliers experience.
Share real demand data upstream so each tier reacts to the customer, not to the tier below it.
Smooth your own ordering with steadier reorder rules so you stop injecting whip into the chain.

definitions.def: The direction toward raw materials and suppliers, away from the end customer. The further upstream a tier sits, the more distorted the demand signal it receives.
The size of the change in actual end-customer demand, usually expressed as a percentage move up or down over a period.
The number of hand-off layers between real demand and a given supplier, each of which magnifies the swing as it passes upward.

content.equation: Upstream Swing = Demand Swing × Tiers Amplified
content.equation_example: Real customer demand moves just 10%, and there are 4 tiers between the customer and your raw-material supplier. Upstream Swing = 10% × 4 = a 40% swing hitting the top of the chain. The more tiers and the less shared data, the more a small blip becomes a violent surge; a short, transparent chain keeps the swing small.
content.closing_statement: Trace a reaction back to the real signal before you amplify it, in supply chains and in life.
content.question: Are your upstream orders swinging far harder than your actual end-customer demand is?

caption:
Principle Nº 335: Bullwhip Effect

Small swings in customer demand amplify into huge swings the further upstream you go.

Ask yourself: Are your upstream orders swinging far harder than your actual end-customer demand is?
